source_title: #422 – Mark Cuban: Shark Tank, DEI & Wokeism Debate, Elon Musk, Politics & Drugs

paragraphs.speaker: lex
paragraphs.name: Lex Fridman
paragraphs.text: You've started many businesses, invested in many, heard pitches on Shark Tank. What makes a great entrepreneur?

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: Somebody who's curious, who wants to keep learning because business is ever-changing. Somebody who's agile because as you learn new things and the environment changes, you have to adapt. And somebody who can sell. No business has ever survived without sales. If your product isn't the most important thing and you're not dying to tell people about it, you're not going to succeed.

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: Selling is just helping. I put myself in the shoes of another person and ask: Can I help this person? From selling garbage bags door-to-door at twelve, asking 'Do you use garbage bags? Let me save you time, I'll bring them to your house,' to streaming—we broke down geographic barriers—to Cost Plus Drugs. We sell trust. We buy drugs, sell drugs, but we add transparency.

paragraphs.speaker: lex
paragraphs.name: Lex Fridman
paragraphs.text: Are you born with sales ability or can you develop it?

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: You can definitely develop it. Selling garbage bags was easy—who's going to say no to a twelve-year-old saving them time and money? You don't have to be a born salesperson to ask those questions, but you have to put in the time to learn that business. I read like a maniac. Give me any type of business and it'll take me two seconds to figure out how they make money and how I can make it more productive.

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: My dad did upholstery on cars, got up every morning at seven, came back exhausted. I learned to be nice, caring, accepting. He didn't have business experience, but he would bring me to sweep floors—not to learn the business, but to learn how backbreaking that work was. He lost an eye in an accident. The only thing he wanted from my brothers and me was to never work like that, to go to college, to figure it out.

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: When I first started a business, I was more of a yeller, more of an asshole than I wish I would have been. I had no patience for somebody not using common sense. I was always go, go, go, trying to be successful, to get independence. I hated firing people because it meant admitting a mistake in hiring. I always partnered with people who had no problem with it and delegated that.

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: Taking the first leap to start a business—I was sleeping on the floor, six guys in a three-bedroom apartment, so I couldn't go any lower. There was no downside. I was twenty-five when we started MicroSolutions. I'd just gotten fired. I'm a lousy employee. I started going to prospects, asking them to front money for software. A company called Architectural Lighting put up five hundred dollars, and that's how I started.

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: People ask me what kind of business should I start—that tells me you're not ready. You have to go through a process: have an idea, talk to friends and family, Google it, see no one else is doing it without thinking ten companies already went out of business trying it. Then people stop because that next step means changing your life. If you want to take that step, you have to deal with the consequences. If you've got nothing, it's the perfect time. If you have a job, a mortgage, a family, you've got to save money.

paragraphs.speaker: lex
paragraphs.name: Lex Fridman
paragraphs.text: What is it about America that has so many people acting on the dream of starting a business?

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: We've got a culture of consumption and more. Creating a business gives you the greatest potential upside and leverage on your time, but also the most risk. Joe Biden said to me at an entrepreneurship conference: the one thing that separates us is entrepreneurship. We're the most entrepreneurial country in the world, and no one else is even close. When you look at the origin of the biggest companies, there's an American origin story. We see those Horatio Alger stories—Bezos, Jobs—and those are the people we want to copy.

paragraphs.speaker: mark
paragraphs.name: Mark Cuban
paragraphs.text: That's the whole reason I do Shark Tank. Every single minute of every episode we celebrate the American dream. We're amazing advertising for it, shown around the world. Every time somebody walks onto that carpet from Dubuque, Iowa or Ketchum, Idaho, that sends a message to every seven, eight, nine, ten, twelve-year-old kid watching: if they can do it, you can do it. Kids walk up to me now and say, 'I started watching you when I was five or ten, and I started a business because I learned from Shark Tank.'
